name: Thatched cob cottage
description: Traditional cottage built from cob (chalk, clay and straw) under a thatched roof, common in rural chalkland villages.

name: Flint-and-brick cottage
description: Cottage with knapped flint walls dressed with brick, characteristic of chalk-belt villages.

name: Georgian townhouse
description: Elegant, symmetrical period townhouse with sash windows, common in market towns and cities.

name: Victorian terrace
description: Brick terraced housing with bay windows, widespread in towns and on city fringes.

name: 1930s semi-detached
description: Inter-war semi-detached house, often with bay windows and generous gardens.

name: New-build estate home
description: Modern estate housing built to current energy standards, common around growing towns and garrison areas.

name: New Forest cottage
description: Regional vernacular cottage built within or on the edge of the New Forest, often timber-framed or brick with characteristic cottage gardens.

name: Garrison / Service Family Accommodation
description: Purpose-built military family housing in and around garrison towns; mostly leased by the MoD but a portion released for civilian sale.
